2603.29617.title: Convergent Representations of Linguistic Constructions in Human and Artificial Neural Systems
2603.29617.paper_summary: Understanding how the brain processes linguistic constructions is a central challenge in cognitive neuroscience and linguistics. Recent computational studies show that artificial neural language models spontaneously develop differentiated representations of Argument Structure Constructions (ASCs), generating predictions about when and how construction-level information emerges during processing. The present study tests these predictions in human neural activity using electroencephalography (EEG). Ten native English speakers listened to 200 synthetically generated sentences across four construction types (transitive, ditransitive, caused-motion, resultative) while neural responses were recorded. Analyses using time-frequency methods, feature extraction, and machine learning classification revealed construction-specific neural signatures emerging primarily at sentence-final positions, where argument structure becomes fully disambiguated, and most prominently in the alpha band. Pairwise classification showed reliable differentiation, especially between ditransitive and resultative constructions, while other pairs overlapped. Crucially, the temporal emergence and similarity structure of these effects mirror patterns in recurrent and transformer-based language models, where constructional representations arise during integrative processing stages. These findings support the view that linguistic constructions are neurally encoded as distinct form-meaning mappings, in line with Construction Grammar, and suggest convergence between biological and artificial systems on similar representational solutions. More broadly, this convergence is consistent with the idea that learning systems discover stable regions within an underlying representational landscape - recently termed a Platonic representational space - that constrains the emergence of efficient linguistic abstractions.
2603.29617.paper_summary_zh: 理解大腦如何處理語言結構是認知神經科學和語言學中的一個核心挑戰。最近的計算研究顯示，人工神經語言模型自發地發展出區分的論證結構建構（Argument Structure Constructions, ASCs）表示，並生成關於何時以及如何在處理過程中出現建構層級信息的預測。本研究使用腦電圖（EEG）測試這些預測在人類神經活動中的表現。十位母語為英語的受試者聆聽了200個合成生成的句子，涵蓋四種建構類型（及物動詞、雙及物動詞、引起運動、結果性）同時記錄神經反應。使用時間-頻率方法、特徵提取和機器學習分類的分析顯示，建構特定的神經標記主要在句子末尾位置出現，該位置的論證結構變得完全消歧，並且在阿爾法波段中最為明顯。成對分類顯示出可靠的區分，特別是在雙及物動詞和結果性建構之間，而其他對則存在重疊。關鍵是，這些效應的時間出現和相似性結構與基於重複和Transformer的語言模型中的模式相似，在這些模型中，建構表示在整合處理階段出現。這些發現支持語言建構在神經上被編碼為不同的形式-意義映射的觀點，與建構語法一致，並暗示生物系統與人工系統在類似的表徵解決方案上存在收斂。更廣泛地說，這種收斂與學習系統在潛在表徵景觀中發現穩定區域的想法一致——最近被稱為柏拉圖表徵空間——這限制了有效語言抽象的出現。
2603.29617.author: Pegah Ramezani et.al.
2603.29617.authors: Pegah Ramezani, Thomas Kinfe, Andreas Maier, Achim Schilling, Patrick Krauss
